steps.title: Find the main claim and the contrast
steps.content: The passage opens by noting that solid fuel use in Egypt dropped sharply — often explained by the energy-ladder model, which ties fuel choice mainly to household income. The word however signals that what follows pushes back on that explanation. Tesfaye and Lund’s study calls the model “reductive” and cites multiple factors beyond income. So the text’s purpose is to dispute a common explanation, not simply describe fuel patterns or critique prices.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices, confirm the match
steps.content:
• Choice A — To contrast patterns in household fuel use in two countries.: Egypt is only a brief setup; Ethiopia is the focus, and there is no country-vs.-country contrast. ✗
  • Choice B — To account for the effect of fuel use on household income.: The passage is about what drives fuel choice, not about how fuel use affects income. ✗
  • Choice C — To critique a frequently used model of changes in household fuel prices.: The energy-ladder model is about income, not prices; this distorts the passage. ✗
  • Choice D — To challenge a common explanation for households' fuel choices.: The text uses the Ethiopian study to show the income-based energy-ladder model is reductive — directly challenging a common explanation for fuel choices. ✓
That is the correct answer.
